ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for fish-and-egg salad with customisable toppings and no sauce. Mapped to the broader 'salad food product' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed salad with fish and egg
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for customisable fish-egg salad without sauce. A0CKP (composite mixed salad) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor; no code invented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept maps precisely to this customisable salad variant; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: grilled or poached fish (preparation method variable)
main_ingredients: fish (species variable — e.g. salmon, tilapia, mackerel)
four variable toppings (e.g. cherry tomato, cucumber, corn, carrot, beetroot, bell pepper)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, representing a full plate of mixed greens topped with a portion of fish, one egg, and four vegetable toppings with no sauce, as typically served at Singapore hawker centres and café salad bars.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Salad with Egg (4 Toppings), No Sauce — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for food code F08010027. All per-100g values preserved as reported. Saturated fat not separately reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite analogue (mixed salad with fish and egg, no dressing) and documented in notes. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and water not reported by HPB for this entry; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed salad with protein
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific customisable fish-egg salad. GI estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on analogous entries from the University of Sydney GI Database for mixed green salads with lean protein and no dressing (GI range 20–40). The very low carbohydrate content (2.54 g/100g) and high fibre (1.89 g/100g) strongly attenuate glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (32 × 7.62) / 100 ≈ 2.4, confirming negligible glycaemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 285 mg/100g — below the 400 mg/100g threshold; no added sauce eliminates the primary sodium vector in salad dishes
hypertension.guidance: This no-sauce variant is the lowest-sodium option in the egg-salad series at 285 mg/100g; individuals managing hypertension should request minimal salt during fish preparation and may use lemon juice or fresh herbs as sodium-free flavour enhancers to keep total sodium within the 2,000 mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.54 g/100g), low sugar (2.08 g/100g), high protein (5.63 g/100g), and 1.89 g/100g dietary fibre produce a negligible glycaemic load (GL ≈ 2.4 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycaemic load of approximately 2.4 per 300 g serving and a high-protein, high-fibre composition, this dish is an excellent glycaemically stable meal choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; ensure fish is grilled or poached rather than battered, and monitor egg intake if managing concurrent dyslipidaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (3.05 g/100g), estimated low saturated fat (~0.72 g/100g), no sauce, and potential omega-3 contribution from oily fish species support a cardioprotective dietary pattern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish aligns with heart-healthy dietary guidelines due to its low saturated fat, absence of added sauce, and lean protein from fish; selecting oily fish species such as salmon, mackerel, or sardine maximises omega-3 (EPA/DHA) intake, which is associated with reduced triglycerides and cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: No sauce, diverse vegetable toppings, fish protein with potential omega-3, and very low saturated fat provide a strong anti-inflammatory dietary profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose oily fish (salmon, mackerel, sardine) for maximum omega-3 benefit and avoid nightshade toppings (tomato, bell pepper, eggplant) if nightshade sensitivity is present, as solanine may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Salad with Egg (4 Toppings), No Sauce (HPB food code F08010027) is a freshly assembled Western-style salad dish served at Singapore hawker centres, café salad bars, and health-food counters. The dish consists of a base of fresh mixed salad greens topped with a portion of cooked fish (species variable — commonly tilapia, salmon, or mackerel), one whole egg (hard-boiled or soft-boiled), and a customer's choice of four vegetable toppings such as cherry tomatoes, cucumber slices, shredded carrot, corn kernels, beetroot, or bell pepper strips. No sauce or dressing is added, making this the lowest-calorie and lowest-sodium variant in the egg-salad series. The texture profile combines the tender flakiness of cooked fish, the firm-yet-yielding texture of egg, and the crisp freshness of raw vegetables. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (60 kcal/100g), very low in carbohydrates (2.54 g/100g), moderate in protein (5.63 g/100g), and provides 1.89 g/100g dietary fibre. Sodium is 285 mg/100g — well within safe limits for hypertension management. The glycaemic load per standard 300 g serving is approximately 2.4, making it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The absence of sauce and the potential omega-3 contribution from oily fish species make this dish suitable for cardiovascular disease prevention and anti-inflammatory dietary patterns relevant to rheumatoid arthritis. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Common allergens are fish and egg.
common_user_queries: Is fish salad with egg healthy?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (food code F08010027) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous composite; all other extended micronutrients set to -1 where not reported by HPB. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed protein salads with no dressing, clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.72 g/100g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Salad, vegetable tossed, without dressing, with egg and fish' (NDB 06016) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. This value should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement when available. 2. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this customisable mixed salad are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F08010027) as of 2026-04-16, and no sufficiently close analogue in USDA FoodData Central provides a complete micronutrient match for this specific dish configuration. 3. Fish species is not fixed in this generic record; omega-3 content will vary substantially depending on whether white fish (tilapia, cod) or oily fish (salmon, mackerel, sardine) is used. 4. Halal status is conditionally true assuming fish and egg sourced from halal-certified suppliers; kosher status not confirmed. 5. The four toppings are customer-selected and variable; the per-100g values represent the HPB canonical composite average across typical topping combinations. 6. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16.
